item.title: 2019-12-23 reviewing cats as bloodsport
item.description: <p>Welcome to Issue #1 of R4, my periodic review of reviews of review reviews. So, like, people write reviews of something, then other people review them, then someone collects <em>them</em> — and I review those collections! Got it? Good. Issue #1 is dedicated to the Digg’s collection of “The Funniest, Meanest Lines From The Very Bad Reviews For The ‘Cats’ Movie.” REVIEW: The last few days have been a Garden of Earthly Delights when it comes to hilarious, imaginative things to say about CATS. Somehow, Digg succeeded in the statistically impossible task of collecting lines that are emphatically neither. RATING: 💩 —— (1 out of 5). The only reason Digg gets that 1 is so you’ll know the rating system uses poops. If there were a way to do a fecal transplant and <em>insert</em> poops back into Digg so it could get a negative-poop rating, they’d deserve it for this (wait for it…) piece of sh*t.</p>

item.title: 2020-04-22 student–administrator ratio
item.description: <p>Dear faculty — In the coming months you’ll hear “student–faculty ratio” mentioned, often in decisive ways. For example, administrators will tell you that, with dropping enrollments, the need to maintain some ratio will require laying off adjuncts or even full-timers. Before that happens, you should introduce the idea of your school’s “student–administrator ratio.” Do it early and often, and get it on agendas wherever you can. Good luck! Ted</p>

item.title: 2020-05-20 extrapolating covid numbers
item.description: <p><a href="https://www.washingtonpost.com/health/government-report-predicts-covid-19-cases-will-reach-200000-a-day-by-june-1/2020/05/04/02fe743e-8e27-11ea-a9c0-73b93422d691_story.html" class="external">“A draft government report projects covid-19 cases will surge to about 200,000 per day by June 1, a staggering jump that would be accompanied by more than 3,000 deaths each day.”<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> The US is currently seeing ~24k new cases/day with a mortality rate of ~2k/day. So, according to this story, if new cases jump by ~800%, deaths will increase by just 50%. That doesn’t sound very credible, does it? But if deaths increase proportionally, that’d be close — again — to 15k/day deaths. That’s a Vietnam War’s worth of US deaths every 4 days, the equivalent of WW2 in just three weeks, and a half million in just over a month. If it continued at that rate for a few months — and the only thing hindering it would be magical thinking — that’s getting into numbers associated with words like <em>genocide</em>. I’m pretty clear on this: I think officials who knowingly and deliberately acted in ways that would magnify sickness and death from C-1 on a large scale should be prosecuted, convicted, and punished with the same regard for life that they showed for others — none. Many of you will disagree, but I’m curious about what you think. What would it take for you to decide that this national trajectory is just too much, and that your normal ethical and moral beliefs are no longer up to the challenge?</p>

item.title: 2020-05-12 on bidens and harriss prospects
item.description: <p>One thing jumps out in this puff piece about Biden: Warren and Sanders get sidelined. “Outwardly, at least, Biden appears sensitive to the concerns of progressives,” while he “builds his bridge” to a new generation of — wait for it — centrists. <a href="https://nymag.com/intelligencer/2020/05/joe-biden-presidential-plans.html" class="external">It’s sounding a lot like the veep-for-life is planning on being a one-term president,<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> i.e., saddling his own veep with the Hubert Humphrey problem of having to run both for and against a platform s/he can’t fully claim. I’m sure the GOP would see what a decent that gesture that is and give the 2024 Dem candidate room to grow… 🙄</p>

item.title: 2020-05-16 RNC 50k and covid
item.description: <p>Before C19 we could pretend that the GOP wasn’t a death cult, and that traditional ethics still applied. For example, it wasn’t fine to openly wish for your political opponents to die in large numbers, or to shrug off the collateral casualties that would entail. But the president has explicitly and repeatedly argued that sickness and death should be visited on Democratic strongholds, and has actively pursued policies and programs to achieve that end, without a peep of objection from his party. In short, his administration and party are waging polite biological warfare; the result <em>will</em> be hundreds of thousands dead and tens of millions of lives dragged down by years of economic dysfunction. And I’m supposed to turn the other cheek and indulge in some empty exercise in performative piety about how <em>terrible</em> it is that they’ll hold their convention in Charlotte? F*ck that. <a href="https://www.npr.org/sections/coronavirus-live-updates/2020/05/16/857415236/gop-officials-say-theyre-expecting-50-000-in-charlotte-for-republican-convention" class="external">I hope C19 consumes this building and everyone in it.<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a></p>

item.title: 2020-05-30 trumpian necropolitics
item.description: <p><img src="../../../../blg/new/new_images/2020-05-30-trump-tweets.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed relative|center"></p> <p>The media’s sanctimonious hand-wringing about how America needs an empathetic leader is completely backwards. The truth is that Trump loves death: he fantasizes about it, threatens it, and revels in directing it. Before C-19 this desire of his was mostly imaginary, but 100,000 deaths has whetted his appetite for real numbers. Read these tweets and tell me: Is this a man who hopes that isolated unrest can be calmed down, or does he want to see chaos spread and more cities burn? The answer is clear, which begs a basic question: How much chaos would be too much for Trump? The answer is exponential: the more, the merrier.</p>

item.title: 2020-07-09 on that Harper’s letter
item.description: <p>I’d like to see the signatories to <a href="https://harpers.org/a-letter-on-justice-and-open-debate/" class="external">that Harper’s letter<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> try again, but this time around — instead of discursive triangulation about opinions of opinions — write a manifesto that clearly states what they collectively believe should happen. Sure, fine, free speech, vibrant debate, and all the rest, great — but to what ends? What bedrock principles do they agree on? You may ask if we need another one of those. Well, did we really need a bunch of well-known people to say that everyone should get to say stuff? No. But a bunch of Big Names saying What Is To Be Done? Actually, yeah, we could use more things like that. At least there’d be something specific to agree or disagree about. But the letter they wrote is mostly a high-culture equivalent of GOP press secretaries and their sophistical judo. There’s no there there, or even a there where it could be.</p>

item.title: 2020-08-05 VA schools learn/unlearn from covid
2020-08-06 elite negligence as war crime
item.description: <p><a href="https://web.archive.org/web/20200809105314/https://mobile.twitter.com/sysh/status/1290980055544475650" class="external">This guy’s got the right idea.<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> We <em>will</em> need a new type of jurisprudence modeled on war crimes tribunals but applicable in times of peace — to large-scale, even distributed crimes like ecocide, genocide, and (I think) fraud. The scale of rewards that individuals and collectives can gain from crime ‘at scale,’ the punishments will need to be beyond anything we know in order to outweigh them.</p>

item.title: 2020-08-07 the sudden-not-sudden switch to a virtual semester
item.description: <p><a href="http://archive.today/2020.08.13-155026/https://www.washingtonpost.com/education/2020/08/06/johns-hopkins-switches-virtual-fall-semester-pandemic-worsens-urges-students-not-return-baltimore/" class="external">Same as always: “Couldn’t see that coming.”<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> If you wonder why US higher ed is so off the rails, well, it’s run by the kind of people who barrel headfirst into this kind of stuff. Who could have imagined that a country full of people losing their shit with spring isolation would say f*ck it come summer? Or that Trump’s shambolic non-policies would lead to rampant infection? Or that a planless GOP that’s epipen-level allergic to social welfare would would need to push to ‘reopen’ everything? JFC, you could’ve written this script in February — I <em>did</em> write big parts of it — and yet here we are: just weeks before semesters start, schools are doing sudden U-turns. The sad truth, which is <em>nowhere</em> mentioned except behind very closed doors, is that universities desperately needed the revenues for the coming academic year and ‘optimistically’ waited until the last minute to confront obvious realities — that is, they waited until students were up to their ears in financial and logistical commitments, at the heart of which lies financial aid. In effect, schools have offloaded their cash-flow crises onto students. I get it: I get why, I get how. But I also get that students and faculty should come first, because <em>they</em> are the core mission. Not real estate, not services, not endowments, not anything else. So the next time you hear an edu admin use the word “strategic,” tell them to seriously f*\ck off. Unless they’re admins at MIT, which got the strategy right earlier than anyone else. 🤯</p>

item.title: 2021-01-31 a theoretician to the end
item.description: <p>Unwavering in her dedication to theory 😹</p> <p><img src="../../../../blg/old/social-media/FB_images/2021-01-31-krauss-on-theory.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed relative|center"></p>

item.title: 2021-03-04 mass murderer motives
2021-03-25 mcmansion forensics
item.description: <p>The lead picture for the <em>NYT</em>’s tornado story is a real study in McMansion forensics.</p> <p><img src="../../../../img/2021-03-25-mcmansion-forensics.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt=""></p>

item.title: 2021-04-03 structural aholes
item.description: <p>The <em>NYT</em> has spent the last 45 years cheerleading the rise of the real estate lobbies that have eviscerated much of the city’s cultural force. Now that Manhattan’s become a Disney backdrop for money laundering and tourism (if the two should even be distinguished), the paper is scrabbling around for new angles on the catastrophe. A few days ago it was REAL ESTATE IS TOTALLY BOUNCING BACK UNLESS YOU ACTUALLY READ THE STORY. Today, in a city of nearly 9 million people, <a href="https://archive.today/newest/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/02/nyregion/pearl-river-new-york-landlords.html" class="external">they found two(!!!) heartwarming tales.<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> They could have found countless more small property owners who are fantastic, but no amount of them could obscure the unstated premise that makes this article so hilarious: that the overwhelming majority of landlords are <em>structural *ssholes</em>. And when you strip away that context — which the <em>NYT</em> can’t actually say out loud, of course — the result is the purest propaganda. If you’re looking for today’s equivalent of totalitarian rubbish about some mythical heroic citizen who sacrificed for the good of the factory / people / nation / state, this is it.</p>

item.title: 2021-04-05 WHY? WHY?! WHY?!?!
item.description: <p><img src="../../../../img/2021-04-05-breakfast-theory-before-and-after.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt=""></p> <p>Help me out here, people. The cartoon on the left is a classic, which Mr. Internet says <em>In These Times</em> ran in ‘89. That seems a about right, time-wise. I want to ask what the hell kind of person would look at it and say “That’s hilarious, but it’ll be so. much. better when I redo it artlessly!” That invites a psychological view, but — in keeping with the subject (“But of course!”) — the answer probably spans several disciplines. This kind of thing happens <em>all the time</em>. Why do people do this? How could someone look at the one on the right and think “Now <em>that</em> is how it should be!”?</p>

item.title: 2021-04-12 why privatize now
item.description: <p>I’m all for privatizing anything, as long as the top, say, 6 levels of executives understand that if they fuck up (1) they’ll get the guillotine, and (2) the state can renationalize it on equivalent terms — basically, take all the assets and saddle privatizing investors with all the debt. And, sorry, that applies to past execs too, so no asset-stripping, quitting, and swapping in other heads. So, you want to nationalize USPS? Let’s talk.</p> <p>Theoretical arguments for privatization always omit one key detail: why <em>now</em>? That’s how we know they’re arbitrary ideological hand-waving that serves to obscure large-scale theft: if you said “Fine, we’ll privatize <em>in two generations</em>, the advocates would be a lot less interested. So the question is: how can we either (a) deter large-scale theft, or (b) ensure that large-scale transformations aren’t really theft. Answer: adjust the individual and collective risks.</p>

item.title: 2021-04-23 on green new dealism
item.description: <p><a href="https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/22/opinion/climate-pledge-summit.html" class="external">This is really worth the time.<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> If you read between the lines from Hardin at the beginning to her call to restructure post-WW2 multilateralism at the end, it’s clear that the US will not be able to make the needed changes with its current political and judicial frameworks. If you doubt that, just look at the dysfunctional relations between the feds and the states during the pandemic — including the GOP’s ongoing multipronged efforts to control the government by any means necessary. Radical changes will be needed, from crushing rightist insurgents to a profound restructuring of limitations on liability. The [Green New Deal] is great, but it’s civil — we also need a vision that’s just as ambitious but focuses on <em>criminal</em> law.</p>

item.title: 2021-04-18 obit for a real-life Le Carre character
item.description: <p><a href="https://www.legacy.com/amp/obituaries/washingtonpost/198369749" class="external">This is an amazing obituary<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> for, I think, my step great aunt? She was a pretty astonishing figure — for fans of Le Carré’s Smiley trilogy, she was basically Connie Sachs, but working for the Cousins across the Pond. I never met her, but she was said to be a really delightful person.</p>

item.title: 2021-04-23 Interview with Nabil Ahmed
item.description: <p>“Ecocide as an international crime” podcast with Ute Meta Bauer interviewing Nabil Ahmed. I just finished several weeks of <em>intense</em> work with Nabil developing the next stages of his work. When we started his ideas seemed really impressive, but by the end I was convinced he’s identified a razor-sharp way to slice through (as Clare Boothe Luce’s put it) the <em>globaloney</em> that prevents polluters from being held to account. Ongoing climate disruption depends, amazingly, on a vague cloud of arguments that amount to “Gosh, the world is round so we couldn’t possibly figure out whose pollution went where!” Enough. If we can figure out how to time-share computers, condos, and jets, we can figure out how to apportion responsibility for a global catastrophe. 🎩 to Jussi Parikka for this podcast and the pointer.</p>

item.title: 2021-04-27 the end of the op ed
item.description: <p>For the infinitesimal group of people interested in the relationship between literary genres and physical formats — maybe just me — <a href="https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/26/opinion/nyt-opinion-oped-redesign.html" class="external">this is passingly interesting,<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> mainly as a tombstone. AFAICT, “op-ed” disappeared ages ago.</p>

item.title: 2021-05-10-one of those sentences
2021-05-12 multilevel negation
item.description: <p><img src="../../../../img/2021-05-12-nyt-multilevel-negation.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed relative|center"></p> <p>This headline consists of a half-dozen levels of negation; the ostensible subject, vaccination, is just arbitrary ‘content’ forced into this structure to produce a sense of novelty. This is how we’re taught to structure our thought on a daily basis, and tbh starting to feel about Khmer Rougey about it.</p>

item.title: 2021-05-17 lots of lacks
2021-06-04 catsup nostalgia
item.description: <p><img src="../../../../img/2021-06-04-catsup-dispenser.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed relative|center"></p> <p>I loved these old-fashioned English ‘catsup’ dispensers when I was a kid, so it was fun to spot one in <em>Quadrophenia</em>. You can tell the art director was really chuffed to have it there — the red is way too bright.</p>

item.title: 2021-06-05 nonviolence is like relying on fossil fuels
item.description: <p>Just a few months ago the US was run by a guy who thought big numbers made him great and bigger numbers greater still — and who would’ve delighted in murdering millions if he could’ve. That guy doesn’t represent a serious threat now, but his minions and imitators do — <a href="https://edition.cnn.com/2021/06/24/politics/bender-book-trump-milley-protests/index.html" class="external">and their party is becoming more and more overtly fascist.<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> I know it’s satisfying to eschew violence, but that kind of high-minded preening is like relying on fossil fuels: it only works for so long, and after that we’re <em>all</em> f-d. If you are <em>absolutely sure</em> that the US can escape the threat of rising fascism with peace, love, and understanding, fine; but if you aren’t, then you really need to take some time out to rethink where and how your nonviolent ideals fit into your political beliefs. This isn’t an abstract or theoretical question at all: fascist-enabling legislation is in full gear in the majority of US states, and it could have a decisive impact on the midterm elections in less than 18 months — including giving the GOP multiple pathways to steal its way into a majority in both houses of Congress.</p>

item.title: 2021-07-08 bonesaw mcguffin
item.description: <p>Pretty much the entirety of US media was morbidly fascinated with <a href="https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-9768803/Trump-told-Mike-Pompeo-Ive-never-bone-saw-Saudis-dismembered-Jamal-Khashoggi.html" class="external">that detail<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a>, so singling Trump out for it would be unfair (unless you think he should be held up as a moral exemplar — in which case you might want to game that out). But his witty repartee — “I’ve been in difficult negotiations, but I’ve never had to take a bone saw. Have you ever had to take a bone saw into negotiations?” — says a lot about why that detail was so ubiquitous: it served as a misdirection that made the monstrosity seem more palpable by making it <em>cinematic</em>. If that effect wasn’t the intent behind leaking it, it <em>will</em> be for similar events in the future. In the meantime, Trump and his coterie deserve every single horror they’d so casually impose on others.</p>

item.title: 2021-07-08 how Deleuze and Guattari co-wrote
2021-07-20 economics as ur-discipline NOT
2021-07-24 Dan Doctoroff’s moral anticompass
item.description: <p>Whatever Dan Doctoroff says or does is reliably backwards: if he’s for it you should be agin it (the NYC Olympic bid, Bloomberg for mayor, Sidewalk Labs), and if he’s agin it you should be for it. In this chapter, <a href="https://www.nytimes.com/2021/07/22/opinion/nyc-outdoor-dining-restaurant.html" class="external">he argues that the best thing to happen to NYC’s streetscape in decades — organic, inventive, varied, as hoc — should be knocked down and replaced by three kind of stuff he likes: a surveillance-capitalist nightmare of standardized, privately administered “utopian” smart-schlock.<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a></p>

item.title: 2021-07-29 acab and police subjectivity
item.description: <p>Hey, people — “ACAB” etc is fine and all, but take a moment to sit with the wrenching testimony of the Capitol cops. They’re giving you a preview of the Truth and Reconciliation process — or maybe Lustration process — the US so desperately needs. If there’s a way out of<a href="https://youtu.be/MrqMf1VG1DI" class="external"> the nightmare of systemic (over)policing,<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> these guys (yes, GUYS) are pointing the way with their shock, despair, and tears. My hunch is that the US is barreling toward a place where we’ll need to see more moments like that, maybe many more — and, more than merely see, commit to. So it makes sense for everyone who claims to care about this to carve out some quality some time to ask themself/ves a basic question: How can we think about the subjective dimension of policing? I wish asking that didn’t play into an apologia for systemic racism, but — imo — what matters most is the future: where do we want to go, and how can we get there?</p>

item.title: 2021-07-31 the post-chaos aesthetic
item.description: <p><a href="https://youtu.be/g63R_Z1h_oE" class="external"><em>This is really worth watching</em>,<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> so 🎩 to Melinda Rackham for the share. Has there been any serious critical writing about this kind post-chaos aesthetic? It’s a way we’ve learned to see since the mid/late ’80s: Gleick’s book <em>Chaos</em> (’87) made it pop, but I remember learning about aspects of it a few years in earlier in Scientific American. Nowadays, this mix of particle-based rendering and a sort of meta-editing — where the visual frame remains the same (or lacks the context needed to tell whether the object or the ~camera is moving) but the temporal frame suddenly lurches from one speed to another — has become standard fare. What interests me here are the variations: disintegration into particles, networks, and other mathematicky structures. This stuff strikes me as a sort of messageless message, and almost (or maybe purely) ideological assertion: that the world is just endless morphology. Not this piece by Kurokawa per se, more this mode of rendering a vision of reality, a sort of spectacular nihilism. This form is technocratic, and in obvious ways it also builds on the aesthetic of mutability at the heart of psychedelic aesthetics — think of the lettering and layout of late-’60s San Fran concert posters. The revived interest in psychedelics, which the Bay Area has been driving once again fits right in, and in non-obvious ways — for example, the interest in psylocybin to compensate for a sort of widespread ‘computer-aided’ depression. One BIG problem with the aesthetic at work in this video (again, the general aesthetic, not this specific piece) is that math doesn’t offer a comprehensible human narrative: no beginning, no middle, no end. In this piece, the sound score tries to add that (too heavy-handed, imo), but it doesn’t really go anywhere, it just intensifies. Someone better at listening than I am could surely find much more, but to me it just sounds like an electro-industrial fugue. Anyway, if any of you know of some serious thinking about the rise of this aesthetic, pointers would be much appreciated.</p>

item.title: 2023-03-11 NYT’s five stages of acceptance
item.description: <p>We need a Kübler-Rossian scheme to describe the <em>NYT</em>’s gradual realization that <a href="https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2023/03/11/upshot/office-conversions.html" class="external">the “idea” of converting Manhattan office buildings to residential space is dead.<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> The first stage is denial: “NYC is coming back!” The second stage is closer to bargaining: “But the mayor and the governor are tweaking laws to make it easier.” We’re on the cusp of the third, depression-like stage now: “It’s pretty much technically impossible.” The fourth stage will focus on the financial obstacles: broke owners won’t be willing or able to take huge risks on isolated projects, and getting them to act collectively? <em>LOLOLO</em>. The fifth stage will be accepting that underutilized and poorly maintained “Class A” real estate, with all its intertwingled “smart” this and “BIM” that, will be a disaster. Except, by the time the <em>NYT</em> finally gets to that stage, the MTA (read: subways) will be well and truly fucked.</p>

item.title: 2021-08-02 reading The Fifth Risk
item.description: <p>I just finished <a href="https://isbn.nu/9781324002642" class="external">Michael Lewis’s book <em>The Fifth Risk</em><svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a>, which is a bit strange to read now, because the ostensible subject is the lunatic incompetence of Trump and his claque. But it turns out to have a sort of redemptive aspect as the focus shifts from their destruction to what, in their ignorance and belligerence, they tried to destroy — in particular, Commerce (esp the NOAA), Energy, and the FDA. I had no idea of the scope of the FDA’s responsibilities, which is completely mind-blowing. It’s a pity the book will mostly be consigned to the dustheap of transient politics, because in retrospect it’s something very different — a well-written, useful, and positive view of the US’s professional civil service.</p>

item.title: 2021-08-04 more than just an acknowledgment
item.description: <p>A genuine question, not snark: land acknowledgments have become common among progressive arts and culture organizations, but do these organizations have actual follow-up procedures for responding to, say, someone at the door claiming Native American heritage and requesting free entry? My point isn’t to suggest that such a transaction could be equivalent in any way to legacies of genocide — just no. Instead, it’s that the failure to follow through with such an obvious procedure makes the acknowledgments seem hollow in one respect, their abstraction. I recognize that acks have many other aspects — for example, as reminders for all involved to contextualize their experiences, to consider the programming in new ways, to think about others’ sacrifices, and so on. Still, it seems to me that it’d be hard to present an affirmative argument that organizations <em>should</em> make land acknowledgments but have no obligation to consider what those words might mean concretely. And every kind of organization should attend to the practical dilemmas its front-line staff might face. Note: I’m not even getting into <em>what</em> those procedures might be — that’s a whole other kettle of fish. I’m just wondering <em>if</em> these procedures have been thought out in some cases.</p>

item.title: 2021-08-6 eery and eerily
2024-11-24 crime is the new governance
2024-08-10 on ethics and robotics
item.description: <p><img src="../../../../blg/old/social-media/FB_images/prisons-and-hitchhikers.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="relative wmed|left"></p> <p>FB has decided this is one of those images I need to see again and again and again, via various “unsolicited pix” groups that claim to be about this or that but really are just firehoses of rando-pandering surveillance probes. In this case, it always comes with a useless comment like “LOLZ” or some equivalent, the social media equivalent of <em>explaining a joke</em> by <em>explaining there is a joke</em>. Except there isn’t a joke: combinations of signs like this have been bog-standard across the US landscape for decades. None of which is very interesting, but what is interesting (imo): visual techniques for triggering crude reactions — some image that invites you to add 2+2, notes a contradiction, accuses someone of “hypocrisy,” etc — are ubiquitous on social media. And,not surprisingly, accusations of hypocrisy are on the same level as those anonymous bro-logic posts aimed at pointing out the “contradictions” of progressive policy, like “plants depend on decomposing animal matter so vegans are really carnivores!!!” or “electric vehicles still need to be charged so!!!” — lamprey-level cognition at best. So next time you feel the urge to point out that some politician said or did something “hypocritical,” maybe don’t. It’s not just passively dumb, it’s actively, infectiously stupid: people are inconsistent, change their minds, are self-serving, etc. These qualities are <em>human</em>. The assumptions that these qualities are somehow <em>bad</em>, and that evidence of them is necessarily <em>damning</em> — that isn’t ethics, it’s robotics.</p>

item.title: 2024-08-18 so many new moons
item.description: <p>Apparently there’s a whole series of big moons coming up, and I saw my first claim that so-and-so Native American tribe called this a “[such-and-such] moon.” The details don’t matter, but internet-era fantasies — of made-up “wolf moons,“ “strawberry moons,” etc — being legitimized as <em>heep-um authentic</em>, that’s a predictable devolution. We should start calling them “router moons,” “wifi moons,” “document moons,” because that’s a lot closer to the truth. <a href="../../../.././../../../tags/suburbanlegend" class="tag-link internal alias" data-slug="tags/suburbanlegend">suburbanlegend</a></p>

item.title: 2024-08-19 third parties and doing good
item.description: <p><img src="../../../../blg/old/social-media/FB_images/third-parties-and-doing-good.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="relative wmed|left"></p> <p>This sounds like a soundbite, i.e., a short statement extracted from a larger context, but 18YO [i.e., my eighteen-year-old daughter] has turned me on to the staggering number of Kids Today for whom this is pretty much the sum total of their grasp of presidential politics: they seem to think the <em>only</em> thing the Dems have done is be <em>Against Trump</em>. While I can’t say I’ve actually talked with staggering number of KTs, my sense is that an alarmingly large segment of them are mostly or entirely clueless about what the Biden admin has actually gotten done. The admin is responsible for that: their “messaging,” when it’s existed at all, has been beyond inept — a profound mistake given how deeply Trump turned many people away from politics at all. But anyone who thinks they’ll “do good” by voting for a third-party clown is a f*cking idiot to a degree that negates even the best intentions. And it takes a <em>lot</em> to get me to say things like this.</p>

item.title: 2024-08-20 gramsci stats overflow
item.description: <p><img src="../../../../blg/old/social-media/FB_images/clown-gramsci-overflow.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="relative wmed|left"></p>

item.title: 2024-08-20 phil donahue rip
2024-08-22 17th c conceptual art
2024-08-23 illustration and computation
item.description: <p><img src="../../../../blg/old/social-media/FB_images/steinberg-city-73.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="relative wmed|left"></p> <p>I don’t understand why everyone isn’t utterly fascinated by illustration, in particular by ’70s illustration. Why the ’70s? Because that was when the potentials of computation were really beginning to permeate society, but the imaging technologies weren’t there yet, so there was a very palpable gap between technical imaginaries and, as art historians say, the hand — a useful frame for looking at this by Saul Steinberg from Feb ’73. I don’t understand why everyone isn’t utterly fascinated by illustration, in particular by ’70s illustration. Why the ’70s? Because that was when the potentials of computation were really beginning to permeate society, but the imaging technologies weren’t there yet, so there was a very palpable gap between technical imaginaries and, as art historians say, the hand — a useful frame for looking at this by Saul Steinberg from Feb ’73.</p>
